text: Thank you very much. I'm Vahag Nicolian from the Oregon Health and Science University, and I'm presenting on behalf of my team. This is a case of a robotic transabdominal preperitoneal inguinal hernia repair in a patient with an infrapubic penile prosthesis. Disclosures, Dr. Orenstein and myself serve as instructors and consultants for a variety of groups, though these are not relevant to today's conversation.

This was an eighty-eight-year-old man who presented with a history of atrial fibrillation and infrapubic penile prosthesis and a symptomatic recurrent right inguinal hernia. His hernia history was notable for an emergent right inguinal hernia repair with mesh for incarceration. The operative report described a blown-out floor and a high likelihood to recur. He had had an immediate recurrence within weeks of the operation and had multiple visits to the ED for pain and partial incarcerations.

When operating on patients with infrapubic penile implants, it's important to understand the components and the type of implant in place. This patient had a scrotal pump device with a reservoir placed in the space of retzius. In these particular cases, the tubing connecting the reservoir to the system can exit suprapubic or through the external ring. The term infrapubic penile implant is actually referring to the incision that was made in the skin when the implant was placed, not necessarily related to the tubing. It's a relative contraindication to MIS inguinal hernia repair, and we'll show why.

The CT scan reveals a retro rectus reservoir, an inguinal scrotal hernia, and anterior mesh placement. These visual elements are pertinent to the open approach typically used for inguinal hernia repair in patients with infrapubic penile implants.

However, this patient had had a recurrence, so we elected to move forward with MIS. When performing an MIS repair, it's important to be prepared for scarring that can occur with the reservoir to the bladder, the epigastrics, as well as the external iliac vessels, and have contingencies in place so that you can effectively manage these should they be injured. Mesh placement can be impacted because of the reservoir, so be aware that you may need to modify the mesh or remove the prosthesis at the time of the operation.

The surgeon begins the procedure by developing a peritoneal flap, initially dissecting in the pre-transversalis plane due to the presence of a reservoir. The dissection is performed laterally first, before re-establishing a pre-peritoneal dissection.

The surgeon reduces a moderate-sized inguinal scrotal hernia, revealing the epigastric vessels medially. Upon reduction, a larger lateral pocket is developed to accommodate an extra-large mesh. The medial dissection begins, encountering scarring around the reservoir, which appears to be adhered to the transversalis fascia.

The surgeon carefully dissects and removes the initial scarring layer, revealing the encapsulated plastic reservoir beneath. The capsule is preserved as much as possible, but upon entry, a small amount of serous fluid is typically released.

The surgeon begins retropubic dissection, carefully navigating around the obturator and vas deferens. A critical view of safety is established for the myopectineal orifice, focusing on the retropubic area.

The surgeon reviews the anatomy, showing the midline reservoir tubing, Cooper's ligament, femoral space, indirect space, and surrounding vessels. The reservoir is positioned outside its initial capsule, having tracked over the pubis. The contralateral dissection is further developed, and mesh positioning is considered without removing the reservoir.
The surgeon places an extra large heavyweight mesh with a two-centimeter slit to ensure contralateral coverage, achieving appropriate coverage of the myopectenial orifice (NPO).
The surgeon secures the mesh to the scar tissue instead of Cooper's ligament. The surgical flap is closed in a standard fashion, with no visible intraoperative issues.

The patient did very well. We observed them overnight just to make sure things were alright. The patient was just discharged, required no narcotics, resumed anticoagulation within ten days of the procedure, and in surveillance is doing well with no signs of recurrence.

So in conclusion, Patients who have a penile prosthesis and an inguinal hernia can have a minimally invasive approach to repair. It's important to review the implant and plan for your dissection appropriately. As always, whenever performing a MIS, inguinal, important to not compromise the critical view of the myopectinal orifice. Thank you very much.
